name: Svacchanda-Tantra (position-entry)
dates_notes: This is a position-entry, not an author-entry. The Svacchanda-Tantra is anonymous and is internally datable to the seventh–ninth centuries CE on the basis of (i) Kṣemarāja's eleventh-century *Svacchanda-Tantra-Uddyota* commentary treating it as established scriptural authority, (ii) its position as a developed Bhairava-tantra within the Mantra-pīṭha of the Śaiva canon as classified by Sanderson, and (iii) its absorption of earlier Saiddhāntika cosmological vocabulary. Modern scholarship (Sanderson, Hatley) places it in the seventh–ninth century range.

lineage_polemical.note: Kṣemarāja's *Svacchanda-Tantra-Uddyota* is the principal commentary on the Svacchanda-Tantra and the document through which the tantra's authority is fixed for the Kashmirian non-dual Śaiva reception. The relation is engaged-from-within: Kṣemarāja is doing the non-dual philosophy of a tantra whose ritual programme is more theistically dualist than the Trika.
Abhinavagupta cites the Svacchanda-Tantra extensively in the *Tantrāloka* and treats it as one of the principal Bhairava-tantras of the Mantra-pīṭha, alongside the Mālinī-Vijaya.

core_thesis: The *Svacchanda-Tantra* is the principal Bhairava-tantra of the *Mantra-pīṭha* — the section of the Śaiva canon devoted to mantric ritual centered on Bhairava as the deity of revelation. The work is anonymous, in fifteen *paṭalas*, presenting itself as a dialogue between Bhairava and the goddess on the worship of Svacchanda-Bhairava (Bhairava-as-autonomy, the Self-Willed Lord). Its commitments are three. First, the *Svacchanda*-Bhairava is the supreme deity, distinct in iconography and mantra from the Aghora-Bhairava of other Bhairava-tantras and from the supreme Śiva of the Saiddhāntika tradition; the *Svacchanda-mantra* (the *Aghora-mantra* extended into a Svacchanda-form) is the central liturgical instrument. Second, the cosmological articulation is the developed *adhva* (path) schema — the six *adhvans* (*varṇa*, *mantra*, *pada*, *kalā*, *tattva*, *bhuvana*) as the six articulations of the cosmos that the *dīkṣā* ritual traverses to purify the soul. Third, the ritual programme is articulated in extensive detail: the *dīkṣā* of the initiate, the daily *pūjā* of Svacchanda-Bhairava with mantra-cosmology, and the post-initiatic disciplines including the *yāga* (sacrifice) and the *japa* (mantra-repetition). The tantra's ritual programme is more theistically saturated than the Trika's metaphysically saturated *Mālinī-Vijaya*, and Kṣemarāja's *Svacchanda-Tantra-Uddyota* is the principal interpretive vehicle through which the tantra's ritual authority is reread within the Kashmirian non-dual Śaiva framework. The text is a principal source of the developed Kashmirian Śaiva ritual canon and one of the principal Bhairava-tantras Abhinavagupta's *Tantrāloka* engages.

engaged_works.title: Svacchanda-Tantra
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Anonymous; dated by Kṣemarāja's eleventh-century treatment as established scriptural authority and by the developed character of its Bhairava-tantra apparatus.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Anonymous; ascribed by tradition to Bhairava revelation. The text presents itself as a dialogue between Bhairava and the goddess.

ascription_evidence.description: Sanderson's essays on the Śaiva canon; Arraj 1988 (PhD dissertation on the Svacchanda-Tantra); Hatley's essays on early Bhairava-tantras.

engaged_works.summary: Fifteen *paṭalas* in dialogue between Bhairava and the goddess, articulating the worship of Svacchanda-Bhairava. The opening *paṭalas* (1–4) establish the deity, his mantra, and the iconography. The middle *paṭalas* (5–10) develop the *adhva*-cosmology (the six *adhvans* — *varṇa*, *mantra*, *pada*, *kalā*, *tattva*, *bhuvana* — as the six articulations of the cosmos traversed by *dīkṣā*) and the *dīkṣā* ritual programme. The closing *paṭalas* (11–15) develop the daily and occasional rituals, the *yāga*, the *japa*, and the post-initiatic disciplines. Kṣemarāja's *Svacchanda-Tantra-Uddyota* is the principal commentary and the document through which the tantra's authority is reread within the Kashmirian non-dual framework.

reception_notes: The Svacchanda-Tantra is the principal Bhairava-tantra of the Mantra-pīṭha and the principal ritual-centered counterpart to the metaphysically-centered Mālinī-Vijaya. Kṣemarāja's *Svacchanda-Tantra-Uddyota* is the principal Kashmirian commentary and the document through which the tantra's ritual programme is integrated into the developed non-dual Śaiva-Śākta synthesis. Modern scholarship (Sanderson; Arraj; Hatley) treats the Svacchanda as one of the principal source-texts for understanding the Bhairava-tantra ritual canon. **None of these scholars' works is on disk in our corpus; all cited via field-standard summary.**
